title: Fix URI scheme: drop // authority syntax and absolute-path double-slash hack
description: The current URI format secretx://<backend>/<path> abuses RFC 3986 authority syntax: the thing after :// is not a hostname. Absolute paths require a double-slash hack (secretx://file//etc/key) which is confusing and fragile. Daniel Franke flagged this in code review. Options to evaluate: (a) drop the authority component entirely and use secretx:<backend>/<path>, (b) keep :// but document it as a non-standard opaque scheme, (c) use a different separator such as secretx+file:/etc/key. The chosen format must handle absolute paths naturally without hacks, be unambiguous in config files and shell, and not collide with real URI parsers that treat // as authority. This is a breaking change to every URI string in every caller.
design:
Decision: adopt secretx:backend:/path format (opaque URI, colon separator).

Format: secretx:<backend>:<path>
- secretx:env:MY_VAR
- secretx:file:/etc/key        (absolute path, clean)
- secretx:file:relative/key    (relative path)
- secretx:aws-sm:my-secret
- secretx:vault:mount/path
- secretx:aws-kms:my-key-id?algorithm=ecdsa-p256

Rationale: RFC 3986 opaque URI — one scheme (secretx), no authority component, opaque part is parsed internally as backend:path. Fixes the double-slash hack without fragmenting into 14 separate URI schemes (option C). Directly addresses Daniel Franke's review comment about not using // when the thing after it is not a hostname.

Parsing rule: split opaque part on first colon — everything before is the backend name, everything after is the path (including any leading / for absolute paths). Query params (?field=x) remain as-is.

Migration: mechanical regex replace: s|secretx://([^/]+)/|secretx:\1:| on all URI strings.

Rejected:
- secretx://backend/path (current): // implies authority/hostname, wrong per RFC 3986
- secretx+backend:/path: 14 URI schemes, prefix matching breaks tooling
- Keep // and document as opaque: does not fix the problem, just accepts it
